messages.content:
From the lifelog excerpts you gave me, Dekel Taliaz is mentioned indirectly — you referenced \"the knowledge I received from Adi, that you had psychiatric treatment until five months ago\" and earlier you asked the patient to confirm \"from the knowledge I received from Adi\" (in Hebrew: \"ממה שהבנתי מהידע שקיבלתי מעדי\"). There is no direct mention of Dekel Taliaz in the selected transcript. 

So: based on these lifelogs, I can't identify who Dekel Taliaz is — the name does not appear in the provided entries. Do you want me to search your lifelogs more widely for that name (I can), or would you prefer I check other sources or your contacts/email?

Quick check: Do you mean Dekel Taliaz, CEO/co‑founder of Taliaz (Predictix) in Israel?

If yes, here’s the crisp profile and how to engage:
- Role: CEO/co‑founder, Taliaz. Focus: AI-driven precision prescribing for psychiatry (Predictix). Background in neuro/biotech, digital phenotyping, real‑world evidence.
- What he cares about: peer-reviewed validation, algorithmic transparency, clinical utility, payer/HTA outcomes, and deployment at scale with health systems.
- Why relevant to us: overlap in AI-assisted psychiatric care, potential data/algorithm partnership, and co‑validation in Meuhedet/virtual clinics.

2–3 options to move forward (trade‑offs)
1) Scientific collaboration (predictive prescribing pilot)
   - Scope: run a 6–9 month pilot comparing Predictix‑guided vs TAU in depression/anxiety within our virtual clinics + Meuhedet.
   - Pros: strongest clinical credibility; publishable; payer‑friendly.
   - Cons: longer runway; IRB + data governance overhead.
   - My take: Best for long‑term moat.

2) Commercial integration (limited rollout for high‑need cohorts)
   - Scope: integrate Predictix in intake workflows for SSRI/SNRI selection; measure time‑to‑response, switch rates, and PHQ‑9 delta.
   - Pros: faster ROI signal; improves patient experience; sales story for health plans.
   - Cons: thinner evidence; vendor dependency.
   - My take: Good if we need near‑term outcomes for Oren (ROI/compliance).

3) Data exchange + model benchmarking
   - Scope: de‑identified dataset exchange; head‑to‑head against our models on remission prediction.
   - Pros: de‑risks partnership; informs build vs buy.
   - Cons: legal/data work; no immediate patient impact.
   - My take: Useful parallel track.

Recommendation
- Do 2 + 1 in parallel: a tightly scoped commercial pilot now, while setting up a scientific protocol for a larger RCT‑style evaluation.

Immediate next steps (owner • due date)
- Rami: Send intro note to Dekel proposing a 30‑min scoping call; include 3 pilot KPIs (time‑to‑clinical response, switch rate, PHQ‑9/ GAD‑7 delta). • Tomorrow EOD
- Dekel: Share brief on Predictix validation (top 3 peer‑reviewed results, target indications, integration requirements). • Before call
- Nadav: Draft patient and clinician journey screens for Predictix‑assisted prescribing. • 3 days
- Oren: Validate data-sharing and DPIA checklist; map IRB path in Meuhedet. • 1 week
- Adiel: Draft partnership one‑pager (value prop, differentiation, co‑marketing). • 1 week
- Dekel/Rami: Align on pilot cohort (n≈150), endpoints, and success gates. • Within 2 weeks

Draft intro message to Dekel (you can copy-paste)
- Subject: Precision prescribing pilot – virtual psychiatry + Meuhedet
- Body: Dekel, great to connect. I’m leading virtual psychiatric services at scale (Meuhedet + clinics). We’re evaluating AI‑assisted prescribing for depression/anxiety and would like to scope a 6‑month pilot with Predictix. Proposed KPIs: time‑to‑response, switch rates, and PHQ‑9/GAD‑7 improvements. Can we schedule 30 minutes next week to align on cohort, integration, and evidence plan? Rami

Want me to proceed with a one‑pager for Dekel (clinical evidence for him, ROI for Oren, differentiation for Adiel, user flow for Nadav)?
